IELTS Speaking Part 2: a 4-step method to ace the cue card

IELTS Speaking · 6 min read

Part 2 is the part candidates fear most: you get a topic card, one minute to prepare, and then you have to talk on your own for up to two minutes. The good news is that it's also the most predictable part of the test — and a simple, repeatable method will carry you a long way.

In the long turn the examiner is listening for four things (the official IELTS criteria): fluency and coherence, lexical resource (vocabulary), grammatical range and accuracy, and pronunciation. Notice what's not on that list: having the most impressive story. You're being marked on how you speak, not what you did. That takes the pressure off — you can talk about an ordinary topic and still score Band 7+ if you speak fluently and accurately.

The 4-step method

1. Decode the cue card

Every cue card has a topic line ("Describe a skill you would like to learn") followed by 3–4 bullet prompts ("what it is", "why you want to learn it", "how you would learn it", "and explain how it would help you"). Read the bullets as a ready-made structure — they are literally telling you what to talk about and in what order.

2. Plan in 60 seconds — keywords, not sentences

Don't try to write full sentences in your prep minute; you won't finish, and reading them aloud sounds robotic. Jot one or two keywords next to each bullet. For "why", write a reason. For "how", write a method. That's it. Keywords keep you fluent because you're generating natural language as you speak rather than reciting.

3. Structure the long turn

Follow the bullets in order and signpost each move with a discourse marker so the examiner hears clear organisation:

  • Open: "I'd like to talk about…"
  • Each bullet: "The main reason is…", "As for how I'd go about it…", "What's interesting is…"
  • Close: "So all in all, that's why…"

4. Extend every point

This is what separates a Band 6 from a Band 7+. Don't just state an idea — extend it with a reason, an example, or a feeling. A quick formula: statement → because → for example → and honestly…. Extension is also how you fill the full two minutes without panicking.

A worked example

"I'd like to talk about learning to play the guitar. The main reason I'm drawn to it is that I find music genuinely relaxing — after a long day, being able to play would be a great way to unwind. As for how I'd go about it, I'd probably start with online tutorials and then maybe take weekly lessons to fix bad habits early. What's interesting is that it's a skill you can practise in small chunks, so even fifteen minutes a day would add up. All in all, it's something I've wanted to do for years, and I think it would make my free time far more rewarding."

Notice the discourse markers, the extensions ("because… for example…"), and the natural collocations ("a great way to unwind", "fix bad habits", "add up"). That's the texture of a high-band answer.

Three mistakes that cap your score

  • Memorised answers. Examiners are trained to spot them, and they hurt your fluency score the moment the topic doesn't quite fit.
  • Stopping at 40 seconds. If you finish early, you've lost a chance to show range. Extend, or add a "looking back / looking forward" sentence.
  • Flat delivery. Pronunciation includes stress and intonation. Emphasise the key word in each sentence — it instantly sounds more natural.
